evidence: 9 packages (api, config, exploration, indexer/blacklab, model, query, utils, viz) plus root Main.java — all below the 10-file overload threshold; largest is model/ with 7 files

Package names directly reflect responsibilities: api/ for HTTP handlers, config/ for grammar loading, exploration/ for semantic field analysis, query/ for BlackLab execution, utils/ for shared helpers

exploration/SemanticFieldExplorer.java and exploration/AdjectiveCollocateComparator.java both import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.QueryResults, creating an exploration→query dependency to reach plain result DTOs

model/ contains domain result records (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExplorationResult) while query/QueryResults.java contains parallel result records (WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult family); QueryResults.java's own javadoc reads 'Used by BlackLabQueryExecutor and SemanticFieldExplorer' — acknowledging cross-package use
viz/ contains exactly one file (RadialPlot.java), consumed only by api/SketchHandlers.java — a single-file package with a single consumer is thin
No reciprocal or bidirectional cross-directory edges observed; dependency flow is api/exploration → query → (nothing back up)
All class names use PascalCase, all methods/fields use camelCase — no snake_case drift anywhere in production code.

API package internal helpers (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, RelationUtils) are package-private; public-facing types (HttpApiUtils, WordSketchApiServer) are public — a consistent, intentional visibility pattern.

model package intentionally mixes 5 records (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions) with 2 traditional classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult); the rationale (incremental construction, JavaBeans get-prefix) is documented in Javadoc on both classes.

SemanticFieldExplorer.java import block is scrambled: java.util lines 3-8, then project imports lines 9-11, then java.util.Map/Set/stream lines 12-14, then org.slf4j lines 15-16, then more project imports lines 17-21. Every sibling file (AdjectiveCollocateComparator, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers) groups imports cleanly.

query package visibility is inconsistent: BlackLabSnippetParser is 'public class' while CollocateQueryHelper is package-private 'class', yet both are implementation helpers in the same package; BlackLabSnippetParser has no non-test callers outside the package.

HttpApiUtils has six helpers (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse, requireMethod, requireParam, parseQueryParams) with consistent signatures and a uniform CORS pattern applied in every response method.
All handler methods return a consistent { status: 'ok', ...data } envelope.

QueryExecutor interface documents five distinct execution methods with clear Javadoc, but findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) and executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) are nearly identical in signature and return type (both return List<WordSketchResult>) — the only difference being labelled-position semantics, which is not surfaced in the method names.

/api/semantic-field/explore uses parameter 'top' for max collocates; /api/semantic-field (comparison endpoint) uses 'max_per_noun' for the same concept, and no shared parameter-parsing helper is used.

handleFullSketchForType (surface path) produces per-relation objects with keys {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations}; handleRelationQueryForPattern produces {id, name, collocations, total_matches}; buildRelationResponse (dep path) produces {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations} — three different shapes for the same conceptual object depending on call path.

Class-level Javadoc on WordSketchApiServer lists '- GET  /api/visual/radial   - Radial plot (also accepts POST)' but registerRoutes() registers it via registerPostHandler only — contradicting the documented GET support.

executeQuery returns List<ConcordanceResult> while executeBcqlQuery returns List<CollocateResult> — CollocateResult implements ConcordanceResult, so the return type asymmetry is real but partially mitigated by the sealed interface hierarchy.
BlackLabSnippetParser has 30+ focused unit tests covering all public methods including null/empty edge cases, boundary trimming, XML extraction, and position-based extraction.

SemanticFieldExplorer is tested via a hand-written StubExecutor in SemanticFieldExplorerTest — no live index required, covers compare(), exploreByPattern(), and exploreMultiSeed() with multiple scenarios.
RelationUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, PatternSubstitution, CqlUtils all have dedicated, well-scoped unit tests with boundary inputs.

HandlersTest uses a carefully constructed MockExchange inner class and an emptyExecutor() stub — no coupling to real infrastructure. Covers 400 error paths and a 200 happy path for the explore-multi endpoint.

CollocateQueryHelper — the multi-phase BCQL pipeline (collectHits → scoreHits → rank) that is the core production query path — has zero unit tests. Only reachable via live-index integration tests (BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, DependencySketchTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorTest) that are skipped whenever CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX is absent.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison() and handleSemanticFieldExamples() — two complete endpoint handler methods — have no test coverage at all. HandlersTest only exercises handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti.

HttpApiUtils (parseQueryParams, requireParam, sendJsonResponse, sendError) is used by every handler but has no dedicated unit tests. parseQueryParams has edge-case behaviour (URL decoding, malformed pairs) that is completely untested.

All four live-index test classes use Assumptions.assumeTrue() for graceful skips, so CI always passes regardless of index availability — the actual BlackLab integration path is never verified in normal build runs.
No snapshot tests present; no timing- or ordering-dependent tests found.
BlackLabConllUIndexer (126-line indexer) and ConlluConverter have no unit tests, though they require real file I/O so stub-based testing would require non-trivial test fixtures.
Domain vocabulary is precise and consistent: logDice, collocate, lemma, deprel, xpos, upos, BCQL are used correctly throughout with no generic substitutes.
Record names are expressive: AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExploreOptions, ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult all communicate role and content clearly.

Method names in the query layer are highly specific: exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, buildAndRankCollocates, executeCollocateSearch, trimLeftAtSentenceBoundary — all unambiguous.
The codebase documents its intentional convention divergence: ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult include Javadoc explaining why traditional classes use get-prefix while records omit it.

AdjectiveCollocateComparator (exploration/) does not implement java.util.Comparator — the Comparator suffix misleads Java readers into expecting a functional comparison object rather than a service that analyses adjective profiles across seed nouns.
parseExploreParams in ExplorationHandlers accepts HttpExchange and sends HTTP 400 error responses as a side effect; a parse* name conventionally implies pure extraction with no I/O side effects.
buildBcqlFromPattern in BlackLabQueryExecutor takes both cqlPattern and lemma and performs lemma substitution; the name omits the lemma-substitution step that is the central operation.

strengths: All nine package names precisely mirror their contained responsibility (api, config, exploration, query, utils, viz) — a developer can navigate to the right package without reading file contents
No directory exceeds 7 files; the flat-overload risk (>10 files with mixed concerns) does not apply anywhere in the codebase
Dependency direction is clean and acyclic: api/exploration depend on query/model/config; nothing downstream imports upstream packages
Main.java as the single root-level file is the CLI entry point with genuine high fan-in — not a straggler root
indexer/blacklab/ nesting correctly anticipates future indexer variants while keeping the current implementation contained
package_organization.issue_character: The single structural inconsistency is a split home for result/DTO types: exploration result records live in model/ while query result records live in query/QueryResults.java, forcing the exploration subsystem to import from query/ for plain data types.
package_organization.score_rationale: This is a well-organized codebase: naming is precise, no directory is overloaded, and the dependency flow is acyclic. What keeps the score from reaching 85+ is the dual home for result types — model/ hosts exploration DTOs while query/ hosts equally plain word-sketch result records, and the exploration package must cross into query/ for those types. The viz/ single-file package is a minor secondary smell. Together these are real but contained inconsistencies in an otherwise clear layout, placing the score solidly in the 'solid but uneven' range.

strengths: Naming conventions (PascalCase types, camelCase members) are perfectly uniform across all 30+ files — zero snake_case islands.
Package-private vs public visibility in the api package follows a clear, consistent pattern: entry-points are public, internal handlers are package-private.

The mixed record/class strategy in the model package is proactively documented with Javadoc rationale in both ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult, so it reads as intentional design rather than drift.
Package organisation maps cleanly to concerns: api, config, exploration, model, query, utils, viz — no file is obviously misplaced.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are isolated to two localised files rather than systemic: one file has scrambled imports that break the grouping convention shared by all siblings, and one class has a visibility modifier inconsistent with its sibling in the same package.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase achieves strong uniformity on the highest-impact conventions — naming and module organisation — which pushes the score well above 60. The documented rationale for the mixed-type strategy in the model package is a genuine strength. What pulls the score below 85 is that SemanticFieldExplorer.java has visibly disordered imports compared to every other file in the same package, and BlackLabSnippetParser is declared public while its sibling CollocateQueryHelper is correctly package-private; neither issue is architectural, but together they constitute observable convention drift that a developer navigating the codebase would notice.

strengths: HttpApiUtils applies a uniform CORS-headers-plus-status pattern across all four response methods, so every response path behaves identically without each handler having to remember CORS logic.
All handlers return a consistent { status: 'ok', ... } envelope with no exceptions, making client-side response parsing predictable.
QueryExecutor is a clean, well-documented interface whose Javadoc explicitly distinguishes the responsibilities of each execution method and warns callers about return-type differences.
Error contracts are explicit: every handler uses requireParam and sends typed 400/500 responses; there is no silent null return path from the public API layer.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The issues are not isolated accidents but stem from two recurring patterns: parameter names and response shapes for related endpoints were independently defined rather than extracted into shared abstractions, producing locally coherent but inter-endpoint inconsistencies; and the QueryExecutor interface grew two nearly-identical collocate methods without a clear naming convention to distinguish them.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase's base layer (HttpApiUtils, response envelope, error contracts) is well-structured and highly consistent, which pushes the score above 60. However, three independent coherence problems — different parameter names for the same concept across similar explore/compare endpoints, three non-isomorphic relation-response shapes from the same sketch resource, and a duplicated QueryExecutor surface (findCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern) — would require a developer to audit each endpoint individually rather than relying on a single mental model. These are real 'developer experience drag' issues at the module level, placing the codebase solidly below 80 on the global anchor scale.

strengths: BlackLabSnippetParser is exhaustively tested with 30+ assertions, covering null inputs, boundary conditions, and all extraction paths — exemplary for a parsing utility.
SemanticFieldExplorer tests use a hand-written StubExecutor that cleanly isolates test logic from BlackLab infrastructure, making them fast and always-runnable.
Live-index tests use Assumptions.assumeTrue() for explicit, documented skips rather than hard-coded flags or ignored annotations.
HandlersTest uses a thorough MockExchange inner class to test HTTP-layer logic without an actual server, covering both error (400) and success (200) paths.
Pure algorithmic utilities (LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, RelationUtils, PatternSubstitution) all have dedicated, well-scoped unit tests.
test_strategy.issue_character: Coverage is strong for leaf-level utilities and pure algorithmic code, but the multi-phase query execution pipeline (CollocateQueryHelper) and two handler methods (handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleSemanticFieldExamples) are completely untested outside gated integration tests, forming a concentrated gap in the most operationally critical paths.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase sits below the 'solid but uneven' range (80). Pure algorithmic utilities are well covered, and the stub-based design for SemanticFieldExplorer is genuinely good practice. However, CollocateQueryHelper — the heart of the BCQL scoring pipeline — has zero unit tests, two handler endpoints are unexercised, and the widely-used HttpApiUtils has no tests despite edge-case behaviour in URL decoding. Because all live-index integration tests are systematically skipped in CI, the real query execution path is never verified in an automated build. This is significant drag rather than isolated gaps, placing the score around 55-60.

strengths: Precise domain vocabulary (logDice, collocate, lemma, deprel, BCQL) used consistently, with no vague substitutes like 'score' or 'pattern'.
Record type names (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExploreOptions) accurately describe content and role.
Query-layer method names are specific and unambiguous: trimLeftAtSentenceBoundary, buildAndRankCollocates, extractLemmaWithFallback leave no doubt about behaviour.
The intentional get-prefix vs. record-accessor convention is explicitly documented in two classes, preventing confusion for future contributors.
Private helper records (ExploreParams, CollocateSearch, HitRecord) are scoped tightly and named after their purpose.
naming_quality.issue_character: A small cluster of localised name/behaviour mismatches — one class-level false Comparator implication and two method names that under-describe side effects or a key parameter's role — rather than a systemic vocabulary problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The overwhelming majority of the codebase names communicate intent with precision; the domain vocabulary is strong and no generic verbs (process, handle, do) appear in core logic. Three genuine issues pull the score below 'solid but uneven' (80): AdjectiveCollocateComparator carries a misleading Java type name, parseExploreParams hides an HTTP side-effect behind a pure-sounding prefix, and buildBcqlFromPattern omits the lemma-substitution step from its name. These are real but localised and fixable with renames, keeping the overall quality firmly in the 75-80 band.

issues.summary: QueryResults.java (plain result DTOs) lives in query/ instead of model/, forcing exploration/ to import from query/ for data types
issues.suggestion: Move the result records out of QueryResults.java into model/. Staged plan: (1) Create model/WordSketchResult.java, model/ConcordanceResult.java (sealed interface), model/SnippetResult.java, model/KwicResult.java, model/CollocateResult.java — or consolidate into a single model/QueryResults.java wrapper record if the namespace grouping is desired. (2) Update all import sites: `grep -rn 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.QueryResults' src/` identifies api/SketchHandlers, exploration/SemanticFieldExplorer, exploration/AdjectiveCollocateComparator, query/BlackLabQueryExecutor, query/CollocateQueryHelper, query/QueryExecutor, and several test files. (3) Delete the now-empty query/QueryResults.java. (4) Run `mvn compile` to confirm no missed imports. This eliminates the exploration→query dependency for plain data types.
evidence: query/QueryResults.java contains WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult — all plain data records with no query-execution logic
Its own javadoc states 'Used by BlackLabQueryExecutor and SemanticFieldExplorer', acknowledging that a non-query package is the second primary consumer
exploration/SemanticFieldExplorer.java line 10: import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.QueryResults;
exploration/AdjectiveCollocateComparator.java line 14: import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.QueryResults;

Parallel result records in model/ (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExplorationResult) share the same DTO character but live in the correct package, making QueryResults.java an outlier by comparison

issues.summary: viz/ is a single-file package (RadialPlot.java) with exactly one consumer (api/SketchHandlers), making it a thin isolated namespace
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot.java into utils/ (as a visualization utility) or, if SVG generation is expected to grow, add at least one sibling (e.g. a CollocatePlot or PlotConfig record) to justify the package. Staged plan: (1) Move src/main/java/.../viz/RadialPlot.java to src/main/java/.../utils/RadialPlot.java and update its package declaration. (2) Update the import in api/SketchHandlers.java. (3) Run `mvn compile` to verify. If the viz/ namespace is intentionally reserved for future plot types, add a package-info.java comment documenting that intent.
evidence: viz/ contains exactly one file: RadialPlot.java (confirmed by directory_profiles: file_count=1 is implied by absence from profile list — only RadialPlot.java is in viz/)
grep for RadialPlot importers returns only api/SketchHandlers.java and viz/RadialPlot.java itself — a single external consumer
RadialPlot contains no interface or extension point suggesting future siblings; it is a standalone SVG-generation utility

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.java has java.util imports split across two blocks by interleaved project imports, breaking the clean import grouping used in all sibling files
issues.suggestion: Re-sort imports into the standard groups used by every other file in the exploration package: java.* block first, third-party (org.slf4j) second, project (pl.marcinmilkowski) last; any IDE 'Optimize Imports' action will fix this in a single edit.
evidence: Lines 3-8: java.util.{IOException,ArrayList,HashMap,HashSet,LinkedHashMap,List}
Lines 9-11: pl.marcinmilkowski project imports (RelationConfig, QueryResults, QueryExecutor)
Lines 12-14: java.util.{Map,Set,stream.Collectors} — continuation of the java block
Lines 15-16: org.slf4j.{Logger,LoggerFactory}
Lines 17-21: more pl.marcinmilkowski project imports
AdjectiveCollocateComparator.java (same package) groups imports correctly: java.* first, then org.slf4j, then project imports

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser is declared public while its package-sibling CollocateQueryHelper is package-private, yet both are internal implementation helpers with no callers outside the query package other than tests in the same package
issues.suggestion: Narrow BlackLabSnippetParser to package-private ('class BlackLabSnippetParser') to match CollocateQueryHelper, establishing a uniform visibility policy for internal helpers in the query package; the test class is in the same package path so access is unaffected.
evidence: BlackLabSnippetParser.java line 9: 'public class BlackLabSnippetParser'
CollocateQueryHelper.java line 43: 'class CollocateQueryHelper' (package-private)
grep across src/main shows BlackLabSnippetParser is only called from within the query package (BlackLabQueryExecutor) and from tests co-located in the same package path
Both classes are purely internal helpers; the api, exploration, and other packages do not import BlackLabSnippetParser

issues.summary: Sibling exploration endpoints use different parameter names for the same max-collocates concept ('top' vs 'max_per_noun')
issues.suggestion: Standardise on a single name — 'top' is more concise — across both handlers. Extract a shared parseComparisonParams helper analogous to parseExploreParams so future additions can't diverge again.
evidence: parseExploreParams (used by handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti) reads params.getOrDefault("top", "15")
handleSemanticFieldComparison reads params.getOrDefault("max_per_noun", "50") for the same concept
Both endpoints are documented under /api/semantic-field/* and conceptually do similar things (find collocates for nouns), so callers expect the same parameter names

issues.summary: The /api/sketch resource returns three different per-relation object shapes depending on code path (surface full-sketch vs single-relation vs dep)
issues.suggestion: Define a single canonical relation-result shape (id, name, description, type, pattern, collocations, total_matches) and use it in all three paths. A private static buildRelationEntry method covering both surface and dep types would eliminate the divergence.
evidence: Surface full-sketch path (handleFullSketchForType, non-dep branch) produces {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations} — no 'id', no 'total_matches'
Single-relation path (handleRelationQueryForPattern) produces {id, name, collocations, total_matches} — no 'cql', no 'collocate_pos_group'
Dep full-sketch path (buildRelationResponse) produces {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations} — adds 'description' and 'deprel'

issues.summary: QueryExecutor exposes two nearly-identical collocate methods (findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern) with the same parameter order and return type but different semantics that are not distinguishable from their names
issues.suggestion: If findCollocations is still needed (e.g., for a non-labeled pattern path), rename it to clarify the distinction — e.g., findCollocationsByCql vs executeSurfacePattern. If it is dead code in the current system, remove it from the interface to reduce the decision surface for future implementors.
evidence: findCollocations(String lemma, String cqlPattern, double minLogDice, int maxResults) returns List<WordSketchResult>
executeSurfacePattern(String lemma, String bcqlPattern, double minLogDice, int maxResults) returns List<WordSketchResult>

SketchHandlers exclusively calls executeSurfacePattern; findCollocations appears unused in the API call chain, suggesting it is an earlier API that was not retired when executeSurfacePattern was introduced
The Javadoc explains the distinction but the names give no hint about the CQL-vs-BCQL or label-position difference

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer class Javadoc documents /api/visual/radial as a GET endpoint that 'also accepts POST', but it is registered exclusively as a POST handler
issues.suggestion: Update the Javadoc to '- POST /api/visual/radial - Radial plot' to match the actual registration, or add a GET handler if the endpoint is intended to support both methods.
evidence: Class-level comment: '- GET  /api/visual/radial   - Radial plot (also accepts POST)'
registerRoutes() calls registerPostHandler(server, "/api/visual/radial", ...) only — no registerGetHandler call for this path

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper's multi-phase BCQL pipeline (collectHits/scoreHits/rank) has zero unit tests
issues.suggestion: Extract the scoring and ranking logic (buildAndRankCollocates, extractCollocateLemma) into pure-function helpers and test them with small synthetic inputs. The HitRecord record and freqMap accumulation logic in collectHits can also be tested with a fake HitGroups/Hits stub.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper contains collectHits(), scoreHits(), buildAndRankCollocates(), and executeBcqlQuery() — the core collocate scoring and ranking pipeline used by BlackLabQueryExecutor.
No test file targeting CollocateQueryHelper exists in src/test.
The only tests that exercise this code (BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, DependencySketchTest) are skipped in CI unless CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX is set.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison and handleSemanticFieldExamples handlers have no test coverage
issues.suggestion: Add test cases in HandlersTest for handleSemanticFieldComparison (missing 'seeds' → 400, valid seeds with emptyExecutor → 200) and handleSemanticFieldExamples (missing 'adjective' → 400, missing 'noun' → 400, valid → 200). The existing MockExchange and emptyExecutor() helpers make this straightforward.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers exposes four handler methods; HandlersTest covers only handleSemanticFieldExplore (400 path) and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (400 + 200 paths).
handleSemanticFieldComparison and handleSemanticFieldExamples — including their parameter validation, error paths, and response shapes — are completely absent from HandlersTest.
handleSemanticFieldComparison has its own numeric-parameter parsing and ComparisonResult serialisation path that shares no logic with the explore handlers.

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils has no unit tests despite non-trivial parsing behaviour in parseQueryParams
issues.suggestion: Add a HttpApiUtilsTest covering: null/empty query string → empty map; key-only pair (no '=') → skipped; URL-encoded values → decoded correctly; requireParam with absent/empty key → null return. Use the existing MockExchange pattern from HandlersTest.
evidence: parseQueryParams performs URL decoding via URLDecoder.decode() and silently swallows exceptions with a logger.warn — edge cases (null query, empty query, missing '=', percent-encoded values, duplicate keys) are untested.
requireParam, sendError, sendJsonResponse, and sendOptionsResponse are called from every handler but have no isolated test coverage.
HttpApiUtils is a codebase-wide shared utility; a silent misbehaviour in parseQueryParams would affect all endpoints.

issues.summary: AdjectiveCollocateComparator does not implement Comparator — class name implies a functional comparison object
issues.suggestion: Rename to AdjectiveCollocateAnalyzer or CollocateProfileAnalyzer to reflect that it is a service performing analysis, not a comparison predicate.
evidence: public class AdjectiveCollocateComparator { — no 'implements Comparator' clause
The class is a multi-method service with a QueryExecutor dependency that analyses adjective profiles across seed nouns; it has nothing to do with java.util.Comparator semantics
Java convention: *Comparator suffix implies a Comparator<T> implementation or at minimum a single-responsibility ordering utility

issues.summary: parseExploreParams sends HTTP error responses — 'parse' prefix implies pure extraction without I/O side effects
issues.suggestion: Rename to validateAndExtractExploreParams or readExploreParams to signal that the method both validates and may send an HTTP response; alternatively extract the validation side-effect to the caller.
evidence: private ExploreParams parseExploreParams(HttpExchange exchange, Map<String, String> params) throws IOException
Inside the method: HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...) is called on NumberFormatException
Callers check 'if (exploreParams == null) return;' — a null return signals that a response has already been sent, an implicit contract hidden by the 'parse' name

issues.summary: buildBcqlFromPattern name omits the lemma-substitution step that is the method's central operation
issues.suggestion: Rename to expandCqlPatternForLemma or instantiateCqlPattern(String template, String lemma) to make the lemma-substitution role explicit.
evidence: private static String buildBcqlFromPattern(String cqlPattern, String lemma)
The method substitutes 'lemma' into 'cqlPattern' via %s or prefix-appending — lemma substitution is the dominant behaviour
A reader expecting 'build BCQL from a pattern' does not anticipate that a separate lemma argument drives the output content
